---
title: "How Medications and Medication Regimens relate to Patient Mortality"
author: "Rebecca Basta and Riley Herber"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    self-contained: true
bibliography: ../../assets/dataanalysis-references.bib
csl: ../../assets/american-journal-of-epidemiology.csl
---

The structure below is one possible setup for a manuscript stemming from a data analysis project. It loosely follows the structure of a standard scientific manuscript. Adjust as needed. You don't need to have exactly these sections, but the content covering those sections should be addressed.

This uses [MS Word as output format](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/ms-word.html). See [the Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org/) for instructions on how to use other formats.


```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
# load a few R packages
library(here)
library(knitr)
```


**Authors**  

* Rebecca Basta and Riley Herber 
These authors contributed equally to this work.

**Author affiliations**  

1. University of Georgia, Athens, GA, USA.


{{< pagebreak >}}



# Summary/Abstract
_Write a summary of your project._

_It is recommended to use a structured abstract with Background/Methods/Results/Discussion sections._

{{< pagebreak >}}


# Introduction 
This information is important because it can help healthcare providers better understand the effects of commonly prescribed medications on patient survival. Identifying medications that are
associated with improved or worsened outcomes may guide clinical decision-making, optimize treatment strategies, and ultimately improve patient care and safety.

## General Background Information
_Provide enough background on your topic that others can understand the why and how of your analysis_ 

## Description of data and data source
We are interested in using the MIMIC-IV dataset to explore the relationships between patient medication use, health outcomes, and mortality at Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Boston, MA.
MIMIC-IV contains data for over 65,000 patients admitted to an ICU and over 200,000 patients admitted to the emergency department. Variables within this database include, but are not limited to,
hospital admissions, intra-hospital transfers, laboratory measurements, microbiology cultures, provider orders, medication administration records, medication prescriptions, hospital billing
information, and mortality outcomes.

## Questions/Hypotheses to be addressed
Our primary objective is to examine how different medications and medication regimens relate to patient mortality. This may include analyzing whether certain medications or combinations of
medications are associated with higher or lower mortality rates. To analyze the relationship between medication, use and mortality in the MIMIC-IV dataset, we would first clean and organize
medication and outcome data. We would then explore general patterns by comparing mortality rates across patients who received different medications or groups of medications. Simple summaries and
visualizations could be used to identify trends or potential associations. This exploratory analysis would help generate initial insights and guide more detailed analyses later on.

{{< pagebreak >}}


# Methods 

This study uses the MIMIC-IV (Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care, version IV) database to examine the relationship between medication use and 30-day mortality in hospitalized patients. All data processing and analysis was conducted in R. Full cleaning and wrangling code is documented in `code/processing-code/mimic-processing.qmd` and exploratory analysis code is in `code/eda-code/mimic-eda.qmd`.


## Data acquisition
Data were obtained from the MIMIC-IV database (v3.1), an available de-identified electronic health records database from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, available via PhysioNet. Please see README file in the raw-data folder to get more information on how to gain access to the database. Access requires completion of a data use agreement and credentialing through PhysioNet. The following tables from the `hosp` module were used:

- `patients.csv` — patient demographics including gender, age, and date of death
- `admissions.csv` — hospital admission and discharge records
- `prescriptions.csv` — medication orders per admission

## Data import and cleaning
Raw CSV files were loaded into R and cleaned as follows. Admissions with a length of stay less than 0 or greater than 365 days were removed as likely data entry errors. Medication drug names were standardized to uppercase and stripped of extra whitespace. Drugs were then assigned to one of 13 broad pharmacological classes (e.g. antibiotics, opioids, vasopressors) using keyword matching. For each admission, a binary indicator was created for each medication class indicating whether at least one drug from that class was prescribed during the stay. Full details of all cleaning steps are documented 
in `code/processing-code/mimic-processing.qmd`.

## Statistical analysis
This report presents unadjusted exploratory analyses. Mortality rates are compared across medication classes, age groups, and gender using summary tables and visualizations.

{{< pagebreak >}}


# Results

## Exploratory/Descriptive analysis

```{r}
#| label: fig-mortality
#| fig-cap: "30-Day Mortality Distribution"
library(here)
knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "day-30-mortality.png"))

```

@fig-mortality shows that 30-day mortality is low in this cohort, there are about 1 in 20 admissions that resulted in death within 30 days.

```{r}
#| label: fig-age-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Age distribution by 30-day mortality"
knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "age-distribution-mortality.png"))

```

@fig-age-distribution shows that most people in this study are in the age range of 50-75.

```{r}
#| label: fig-age-mortality
#| fig-cap: "30-day mortality rate by age group"
knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "mortality_by_age.png"))

```

@fig-age-mortality shows that 30 day mortality increased as the age group increased, with the highest mortality rate in the 75+ group.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-gender-mortality
#| tbl-cap: "30-day mortality rate by gender"
#| echo: false

readRDS(here("results", "tables", "mortality_by_gender.rds")) |>
  knitr::kable()
```

@fig-med-frequency shows hows that many particpants used cardiovascular and anticoagulations medicine. The highest used medications were anticoagulants and opioids.

```{r}
#| label: fig-med-mortality
#| fig-cap: "30-day mortality rate by medication class (unadjusted)"
knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "unadjusted-perscriptoin-frequency.png"))
```

@fig-med-mortality suggests that patients who received vasopressors had the highest 30-day mortality rate, followed by opioids. In contrast, patients receiving ACE inhibitors, statins, and calcium channel blockers had lower mortality rates than those who did not receive them, likely reflecting their use in more stable patients. These associations are unadjusted and largely reflect disease severity rather than direct drug effects. Sicker patients tend to receive more aggressive medications and have worse outcomes.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-med-mortality-summary
#| tb2-cap: "30-day mortality rate by medication class (unadjusted)"
#| echo: false

readRDS(here("results", "tables", "medication_mortality_summary.rds")) |>
  knitr::kable()
```

tbl-med-mortality-summary shows mortality rates were higher with patients taking vasopressors, sedatives, and diruetics; this could be because the severity of their illness.

```{r}
#| label: fig-polypharmacy-freq
#| fig-cap: "Number of medication classes received vs. 30-day mortality"
knitr::include_graphics(here("results", "figures", "polypharmacy-frequency.png"))
```

@fig-polypharmacy-freq shows mortality increased as the number of medications increased, this also could be explained by the increasing severity of illness as more medications were needed.


## Basic statistical analysis

_To get some further insight into your data, if reasonable you could compute simple statistics (e.g. simple models with 1 predictor) to look for associations between your outcome(s) and each individual predictor variable. Though note that unless you pre-specified the outcome and main exposure, any "p<0.05 means statistical significance" interpretation is not valid._


@fig-result shows a scatterplot figure produced by one of the R scripts.

```{r}
#| label: fig-result
#| fig-cap: "Height and weight stratified by gender."
#| echo: FALSE
#knitr::include_graphics(here("results","figures","height-weight-stratified.png"))
```


## Full analysis

_Use one or several suitable statistical/machine learning methods to analyze your data and to produce meaningful figures, tables, etc. This might again be code that is best placed in one or several separate R scripts that need to be well documented. You want the code to produce figures and data ready for display as tables, and save those. Then you load them here._


```{r}
#| label: tbl-resulttable2
#| tbl-cap: "Linear model fit table."
#| echo: FALSE
#resulttable2 <- readRDS(here("results","tables","resulttable2.rds"))
#knitr::kable(resulttable2)
```


{{< pagebreak >}}


# Discussion

## Summary and Interpretation
_Summarize what you did, what you found and what it means._

## Strengths and Limitations
_Discuss what you perceive as strengths and limitations of your analysis._

## Conclusions
_What are the main take-home messages?_

_Include citations in your Qmd file using BibTeX, the list of references will automatically be placed at the end_

This paper [@leek2015] discusses types of analyses. 

These papers [@mckay2020; @mckay2020a] are good examples of papers published using a fully reproducible setup similar to the one shown in this template. 

Note that this cited reference will show up at the end of the document, the reference formatting is determined by the CSL file specified in the YAML header. Many more style files for almost any journal [are available](https://www.zotero.org/styles). You also specify the location of your BibTeX reference file in the YAML. You can call your reference file anything you like.


{{< pagebreak >}}

# References
